sections.content:

You are still on the same level.

Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Clicking on the black bar only changed the HUD. Only blue squares produce meaningful changes. I've used 7 actions without solving the level. The charcoal object is currently at cols 56-57, blue squares at cols 60-63.
- Goal model: Perhaps the goal is to move the charcoal object to overlap with the blue squares, or to some other target position.
- Plan: Try pushing the charcoal one more step right to see if it reaches a target position near/overlapping the blue squares.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 8 actions; a strong score needs about 7 or fewer. You are 1.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The charcoal object is now at cols 60-61, right next to the blue squares at cols 60-63. The yellow strip is at cols 62-63. The charcoal object is now overlapping with the blue square area (the lower blue square is at rows 32-35, cols 60-63, but the charcoal is at rows 44-49, so they don't vertically overlap).
I notice something interesting: the gray wall boundary has changed significantly. It now shows `[[1, 0], [1, 19], [31, 19], [32, 20], [32, 59], [35, 59], [36, 60], [36, 63], [43, 63], [43, 60], [44, 59], [45, 59], [45, 58], [46, 57], [47, 57], [48, 58], [48, 59], [49, 59], [50, 60], [50, 63], [63, 63], [63, 0]]`. The concave section has moved to cols 57-59, and the wall now extends to col 63 on the right side.
Wait, I notice the gray wall boundary no longer has the section around rows 27-28 that was there before. Let me compare:
- Before: `[[1, 0], [1, 23], [27, 23], [27, 20], [28, 19], [31, 19], [32, 20], ...]`
- Now: `[[1, 0], [1, 19], [31, 19], [32, 20], ...]`
The section `[27, 23], [27, 20], [28, 19]` is gone. This means the gray wall has changed shape. The black bar area (rows 28-31) seems to have been absorbed into the gray wall.
Actually wait, looking more carefully, the gray wall now includes the area where the black bar was. The black bar is still there (nodes 5, 6, 7), but the gray wall boundary has changed.
Hmm, I'm running out of actions. Let me think about what the goal could be. I've pushed the charcoal object all the way to the right. Maybe the goal is to push it to a specific position where it completes the level. Or maybe I need to push it back to the left.
Actually, let me reconsider the whole puzzle. Maybe the goal is to move the charcoal object to a position where it touches the yellow strip in the black bar? The yellow strip in the black bar is at cols 38-39, rows 28-31. The charcoal object is at rows 44-49. They can't touch directly.
Wait, maybe I should try clicking on the charcoal object itself now that it's in a new position. Or maybe I should try clicking on the white region.
Actually, let me reconsider. I've been assuming the goal is to move the charcoal object, but maybe the goal is something else entirely. Let me think about what other interactions are possible:
1. Click on blue squares: moves charcoal+yellow and gray wall
2. Click on charcoal: only HUD changes
3. Click on yellow gap: only HUD changes
4. Click on black bar: only HUD changes
Maybe the goal is to click on a specific object after moving the charcoal to a certain position. Or maybe the goal is to move the charcoal to a position where it merges with another object.
Let me try clicking on the charcoal object now that it's at cols 60-61.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Charcoal object now at cols 60-61 (rightmost position), yellow strip at cols 62-63. Gray wall has changed shape significantly. Level not completed yet.
Goal model: Maybe I need to click on the charcoal object now that it's in a new position, or try a different interaction.
Plan: Try clicking on the charcoal object at its new position to see if something different happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click on the charcoal object at its new position
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 47, 'col': 60}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("=== Objects ===")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] > 5:
        print(f"  color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

print(f"\nLevel completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over')}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done')}")
print(f"Board changed: {last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
